context: And Nathan said to David, Thou art the man.
point_summary: Nathan's confrontation of David — "Thou art the man" — illustrates that hidden sin, however elaborately concealed, is never hidden from God.

context: David said I want to show the kindness of the Lord. Are there any left of the house of Jonathan?
point_summary: David's desire to show "the kindness of the Lord" to any survivor of Jonathan's house illustrates the believer's adoption into God's royal family, as Mephibosheth was brought from Lo-debar to sit as one of the king's sons.

context: it came to pass after this, that Absalom prepared him chariots and horses and 50 men to run before him.
point_summary: The historical backdrop of Psalm 3 is rooted in 2 Samuel 15, where Absalom's rebellion against David begins with his calculated campaign to steal the loyalty of the people.

context: verses 13 and 14 of 2 Samuel 15
point_summary: The report that Israel's hearts had turned to Absalom establishes the historical crisis—David fleeing his own capital—that forms the immediate backdrop for Psalm 3.

context: verses 13 and 14 of 2 Samuel 15
point_summary: David's urgent flight from Jerusalem in 2 Samuel 15:14 establishes the precise historical crisis—Absalom's coup—that forms the backdrop against which Psalm 3 was written.

context: we skipped over verse number 12 in 2 Samuel 15, but at the end of that verse it says this, and the conspiracy was strong, for the people increased continually with Absalom.
point_summary: 2 Samuel 15:12's closing detail — "the conspiracy was strong, for the people increased continually with Absalom" — supplies the historical ground for David's cry in Psalm 3:1 that his enemies had "increased."

context: The Bible tells us that this man Shimei, he cursed David.
point_summary: Shimei's cursing and stone-throwing during David's flight confirms that Psalm 3's description of widespread opposition was not exaggerated self-pity but an accurate account of real, tangible hostility.

context: The next verse of Scripture tells us that he cast stones at David.
point_summary: The physical assault of Shimei casting stones at David in 2 Samuel 16:6 is cited as concrete historical evidence that David's description of enemies rising against him in Psalm 3 was not exaggerated but literal reality.

context: It tells us that he cursed him, he cast stones and he cast dust.
point_summary: Shimei's cursing, stone-throwing, and dust-casting in 2 Samuel 16:13 confirm that David's distress in Psalm 3 was not exaggerated imagination but documented, concrete reality.

context: But if you go back to 2 Samuel 11, and you read about the sin of David with Bathsheba, the way in which he had Uriah the Hittite murdered, you will find why David lost his joy.
point_summary: David's sin with Bathsheba and his murder of Uriah the Hittite are cited as the reason he lost his joy, establishing sin as the direct cause of joy's departure.

context: Nathan stands before him and he says, thou art the man
point_summary: Nathan's confrontation of David — "Thou art the man" — illustrates that unconfessed sin does not forfeit salvation but does forfeit the joy of salvation, as Psalm 51's cry to "restore unto me the joy" confirms.

context: I think about the Old Testament. I believe it's 2 Samuel chapter nine, Mephibosheth.
point_summary: Mephibosheth's helpless condition and David's unprompted kindness in 2 Samuel 9 illustrate the believer's adoption—brought to the king's table not by merit but by sovereign grace.

context: And Nathan stood before David and said, thou art the man
point_summary: David's attempt to conceal his sin from God failed utterly, as Nathan's confrontation — "Thou art the man" — illustrates the sermon's point that nothing can be hidden from God.

context: Just reading this morning in 2 Samuel, how David and his behavior gave occasion to the enemy to speak against God.
point_summary: David's sinful behavior is cited as a biblical example of how a believer's conduct can give the enemy occasion to blaspheme God.

context: you can read of that in 2 Samuel chapters 15 through 19
point_summary: The historical backdrop of Absalom's rebellion in 2 Samuel 15–19 establishes that David's distress in Psalm 3 was a genuine, serious crisis, not exaggeration.

context: 2 Samuel chapter number 15, this morning, and I'm going to look at a passage of Scripture here really to use as a springboard...
point_summary: 2 Samuel 15 serves as a springboard text, with Absalom's subtle, strategic revolt against David—culminating in stolen loyalties—framing a broader topical message about spiritual readiness in difficult times.

context: so Absalom stole the hearts of the men of Israel
point_summary: Absalom's revolt illustrates how hearts can be subtly and strategically stolen away, setting the stage for the sermon's broader warning about spiritual readiness in times of sudden crisis.

context: Absalom sent for Ahithophel, the Gileadite, David's counselor
point_summary: Absalom's conspiracy against David grew rapidly and broadly, serving as the sermon's springboard context for examining how quickly a crisis can overtake even a greatly used servant of God.

context: the response of those servants in 2 Samuel 15 and verse number 15
point_summary: Contrasted with Peter's self-declared but untested readiness, the servants' response in 2 Samuel 15:15 becomes the sermon's model of genuine, proven readiness before God.

context: Take the truth that we read there in 2 Samuel 15 and verse number 15, when a human king facing some difficult circumstances provides some direction and some instruction to those servants that serve him...
point_summary: Readiness to meet the Lord requires steadfast, total surrender to Christ as King of kings, mirroring the loyal servants of 2 Samuel 15:15 who pledged to do whatever their king appointed.

context: And as we begin to notice and look back at 2 Samuel 15 and 16, and look through those chapters that record for us the event that David is going through,
point_summary: The historical account in 2 Samuel 15–16 confirms that David's description of mounting enemies in Psalm 3 is literal reality, not exaggeration or assumption.

context: And as we begin to notice and look back at 2 Samuel 15 and 16, and look through those chapters that record for us the event that David is going through,
point_summary: The historical record of 2 Samuel 15–16 confirms that David's description of multiplying enemies in Psalm 3:1–2 is literal reality, not exaggeration or assumption.

context: it was told to us in 2 Samuel 15, and verse number 12, and the conspiracy was strong for the people increased continually with Absalom.
point_summary: 2 Samuel 15:12 is cited as historical confirmation that the overwhelming opposition David describes in Psalm 3 was a documented reality, not rhetorical exaggeration.

context: The Bible says this in verse number 31,
point_summary: God's perfection, proven word, and role as buckler are inseparable—David's song presents the trustworthiness of God as the very ground on which his shielding protection rests.

context: As for God, his way is perfect; the word of the Lord is tried: he is a buckler to all them that trust in him.
point_summary: David's use of "buckler" in 2 Samuel 22:31 is shown to be synonymous with "shield" in verse 3, establishing that God's protective role is a personal, trusted reality for the believer.

context: And he said, The Lord is my rock, and my fortress, and my deliverer;
point_summary: David's use of "shield" and "buckler" as synonymous Hebrew terms in 2 Samuel 22:2–3 underscores the personal, protective relationship with God that every believer is called to claim daily.

context: The God of my rock; in him will I trust: he is my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my high tower, and my refuge, my saviour; thou savest me from violence.
point_summary: David's use of "my shield" in 2 Samuel 22:3 is a personal declaration, employing the same Hebrew word as "buckler" in verse 31, affirming God as individual protector for all who trust Him.

context: We use that passage in 2 Samuel 22.
point_summary: God's designation as "buckler" in 2 Samuel 22:31 is shown to be a personal, synonymous parallel to "shield," pressing the question of whether believers consciously enter each day claiming that protection as their own.

context: Very similar to what we just read in 2 Samuel 22 and v. 31. The Word of the Lord is tried. He is a buckler to all those that trust in Him.
point_summary: God's word being "tried" (proven trustworthy) makes Him a reliable buckler to those who trust Him, a truth the sermon connects to the broader theme of knowing who God is and responding in praise and prayer.

context: Can I remind you of 2 Samuel 12 in verse five? Can I remind you of what David said?
point_summary: Sin's deceptive power is illustrated by David's furious condemnation of another man's wrong while remaining blind to his own identical guilt—mirroring Judah's harsh judgment of Tamar.

context: as the Lord liveth, the man that hath done this thing, notice what he says, shall surely die.
point_summary: David's furious call for the death of the man in Nathan's parable illustrates how sin blinds a person to their own guilt while they harshly condemn the same offense in others.

context: Nathan in verse number six stood before David and said, thou art the man.
point_summary: Judah's self-righteous call for Tamar's execution mirrors David's furious verdict against Nathan's parable-sinner — both men condemning others while concealing their own guilt, illustrating sin's power to blind the sinner to himself.

context: the Bible tells us in 2 Samuel 8 and verse number 14, that during his reign, because David was mighty, the Lord was with him and the Lord gave him favor, the Lord preserved him withersoever he went
point_summary: David's military dominance over Edom — placing garrisons there and making all Edomites his servants — illustrates the ongoing, escalating conflict between the descendants of Jacob and Esau across Israel's history.

context: In 2 Samuel chapter 22 and verse number 31, we find this is the song of David.
point_summary: David's declaration at the close of a life marked by both triumph and severe opposition—"as for God, his way is perfect"—grounds the petition "thy will be done" in the conviction that surrendering to God's will is surrender to a flawless way.

context: And I want you to see what David says near the end of his life in 2 Samuel 22 and verse number 31, as for God, his way is perfect.
point_summary: David's declaration at the end of a life marked by both triumph and severe opposition — "as for God, his way is perfect" — is presented as the testimony of one who learned through suffering that God's sovereign plan, however unforeseen, serves His own glory.

context: His way is perfect.
point_summary: God's way is declared perfect in 2 Samuel 22:31, contrasted with human failure, to ground the call to align one's will with God's will in prayer.

context: What were the two sins of King David as they're recorded in 2 Samuel chapter 11? Adultery and murder
point_summary: David's sins of adultery and murder in 2 Samuel 11 are traced back to the heart, beginning with an idle look and a wandering thought, illustrating Jesus' list of evils that proceed from within.

context: And David sent and enquired after the woman. And one said, Is not this Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?
point_summary: David's servant's identification of Bathsheba as a married woman constituted a clear warning the king heard but deliberately ignored before commanding her to be brought to him.

context: He said just make sure you let them know that Uriah is dead. And it's interesting, when David hears that, he says this in v. 25, 'Let not this thing displease Thee, for the sword devoureth one as well as another. Make Thy battle more strong against the city and overthrow it, and encourage Thou him.'
point_summary: David's callous dismissal of Uriah's death — "the sword devoureth one as well as another" — is cited as evidence that his sin had produced a hardened heart problem requiring confession and repentance.

context: in the passage there back in 2 Samuel, we find that it's in chapter 12 when the Lord sends Nathan unto David. And Nathan, the prophet, stands before him and gives him a parable to start and then lets him know, thou art the man.
point_summary: Nathan's confrontation of David in 2 Samuel 12 — "thou art the man" — is presented as the turning point that moved David from hardened self-justification to the genuine repentance expressed in Psalm 51.

context: It was a heart problem in 2 Samuel chapter number 11.
point_summary: The sins catalogued in 2 Samuel 11 are cited as a historical illustration that sinful behavior, across all eras, is fundamentally a heart problem.

context: it was time for the kings to go forth to battle, but David tarried at Jerusalem, according to verse number one
point_summary: David's absence from battle — being out of place spiritually and physically — is presented as the vulnerable position that opened the door to temptation and subsequent sin.

context: He's there, and he sees a woman, according to verse number two
point_summary: David's absence from battle — being out of place — left him vulnerable to the temptation that began with seeing Bathsheba, illustrating how misplaced positioning exposes a person to sin's progression.

context: David sent and inquired after the woman
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba in 2 Samuel 11:3 illustrates how lust progresses into deliberate action, compounded by his being out of place and thus vulnerable to temptation.

context: is not this Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba in 2 Samuel 11:3 illustrates how lust progresses into deliberate action, compounded by his being out of place and thus vulnerable to temptation.

context: the response of verse number three, as David sends and inquires
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba was met with a clear warning—she was another man's wife—yet he ignored it and acted anyway, illustrating how temptation proceeds when its warnings are suppressed.

context: is not this Bathsheba the daughter of Eliam? And this is actually a family relationship to the one who was David's counselor
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba was met with a clear warning—she was another man's wife—yet he ignored it and acted anyway, illustrating how temptation proceeds when its warnings are dismissed.

context: the daughter of Eliam, the wife of Uriah the Hittite
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba was met with a clear warning—she was another man's wife—yet he ignored it and acted anyway, illustrating how temptation proceeds when its signals are suppressed.

context: Thank God for the warning that David was given in verse number three
point_summary: David's inquiry about Bathsheba was met with a clear warning—she was another man's wife—yet he ignored it and acted anyway, illustrating how temptation proceeds when its warnings are suppressed.

context: And you read verse number four, and the Bible says, and David sent messengers and took her
point_summary: David's act of sending for Bathsheba in verse 4 illustrates how ignoring a clear warning—her identity as another man's wife—leads desire into devastating sin.

context: and David sent messengers and took her. She came in unto him, and he lay with her, for she was purified from her uncleanness, and she returned unto her house
point_summary: David's act of sending for Bathsheba in verse 4 illustrates how ignoring a clear warning—her identity as another man's wife—leads desire past temptation into devastating sin.

context: he makes inquiry whether there were any that remained and he finds out about this one, a son of Jonathan, Mephibosheth
point_summary: David's inquiry for any surviving heir of Saul's house illustrates the foundational spiritual truth that believers, like Mephibosheth, begin their relationship with the King as those with nothing to offer, yet are forgiven and welcomed as sons.

context: what do you want with a dead dog such as I?
point_summary: Mephibosheth's self-description as a "dead dog" who yet receives a place at the king's table illustrates the new believer's starting point: forgiven and adopted, with nothing to offer but everything received.
